expectations.text: Looks for relevant patterns in the codebase or skill instructions instead of inventing the design in isolation.
expectations.evidence: No codebase was provided; the agent explicitly flagged 'exploration_skipped_no_codebase' (metrics.json) and grounded the design in canonical Python CLI conventions (Click, src/ layout, pytest). The Relevant Findings section and Next-Agent Kickoff explicitly tell the implementing agent to verify paths and check for existing config.py/settings.py/pydantic-settings patterns before writing code — demonstrating pattern-awareness rather than pure invention. The recommended design (sys.exit + stderr) is justified by referencing the standard CLI idiom. Marginal pass: no skill-instruction citations are visible, but codebase absence was correctly acknowledged and documented.

expectations.text: Creates a concrete file-backed handoff artifact at a path matching the workflow's handoff contract.
expectations.evidence: Artifact confirmed at /Users/adam/.agents/scratchpad/config-validation-module/handoff-plan.md (9,770 chars per metrics.json). File was read and contains complete content. metrics.json records 'created: true' with the matching path.

expectations.text: The artifact content follows a reusable template shape and includes implementation slices, file-level map, and validation plan.
expectations.evidence: handoff-plan.md contains: three Implementation Slices (Core module, CLI integration, Integration test); File-by-File Implementation Map covering four files with action column; and a Validation Plan with TDD, code-simplifier, independent reviewer, automated test commands, manual steps, and review focus. metrics.json confirms all 10 template sections populated.

expectations.text: Artifact metadata fields are concretely filled (no placeholder bracket values) for status, track, and next step.
expectations.evidence: Header fields in handoff-plan.md: Status='ready for implementation', Track='Standard', Recommended Next Step='implement directly'. All three are concrete values. Note: '<package>' placeholders appear throughout file paths in other sections, but those are in implementation details, not the three metadata fields named by this assertion.

expectations.text: Each referenced file in Relevant Findings includes why that file matters.
expectations.evidence: All five entries include explicit rationale: cli.py ('CLI entry point where the validator call should be inserted'); package root ('new module goes here as config_validator.py'); tests/ dir ('location for test_config_validator.py'); pyproject.toml etc. ('check for existing test runner config and package name'); requirements.txt ('no new runtime deps needed (stdlib only)').

expectations.text: Open questions are tightly bounded to truly blocking items and include risk framing.
expectations.evidence: Exactly one open question is raised: required-vars declaration strategy (hard-coded vs. caller-passed vs. config file). It includes explicit risk framing: 'Risk if wrong: If required vars are managed elsewhere (e.g., pydantic-settings), inserting a duplicate check creates drift and confusion.' No trivial or hypothetical questions included.

expectations.text: Provides artifact status in the response with the exact artifact path.
expectations.evidence: response.md 'Artifact Status' section states: 'Handoff plan created at: .agents/scratchpad/config-validation-module/handoff-plan.md' and 'Full path: /Users/adam/.agents/scratchpad/config-validation-module/handoff-plan.md'. Both relative and absolute paths provided.

expectations.text: Includes rationale or trade-offs for the proposed design.
expectations.evidence: Recommended Design section in handoff-plan.md has 'Why this fits better than the main alternative (raising an exception)' with three explicit trade-off bullets: (1) sys.exit is the standard CLI convention vs. exceptions requiring callers to catch; (2) accepting `required` as parameter enables unit-testing without monkeypatching; (3) list co-location makes it a single source of truth.

expectations.text: The Validation Plan section of the handoff artifact contains TDD completion, a code-simplifier refactor pass, and independent code-reviewer review as named discrete steps — not buried solely in the Implementation Slices section.
expectations.evidence: handoff-plan.md Validation Plan has three clearly named subsections: '### TDD Red-Green-Refactor (required — explicit step)', '### code-simplifier Refactor Pass (required — explicit step)', and '### Independent code-reviewer Review (required — explicit step)'. All three appear as top-level headers in the Validation Plan, not embedded in Implementation Slices.

claims.claim: An implementing agent can proceed directly from the handoff artifact without repeating discovery.
claims.evidence: The artifact contains: goal/non-goals, path assumptions, technical constraints, design recommendation with code sketch, ordered slices with file lists, file-by-file map with actions, full validation plan, and a 7-step next-agent kickoff checklist. The <package> placeholders are flagged as requiring verification — step 2 of the kickoff explicitly says 'Check pyproject.toml or setup.cfg for the package name to fill in <package>'.

claims.claim: Standard track is appropriate for this request.
claims.evidence: The feature touches a new module (config_validator.py), the CLI entry point (cli.py), and two test files — multiple files with a genuine design decision. Standard track selection is well-justified.

claims.claim: The recommended sys.exit + stderr pattern is the correct CLI convention.
claims.evidence: sys.exit(1) with print to stderr is the POSIX-standard convention for CLI configuration errors. The alternative (raising an exception) is correctly identified as requiring callers to catch it, adding boilerplate risk.

claims.claim: The four test cases listed are sufficient for unit coverage.
claims.evidence: Tests cover: all vars present (happy path), one var missing, multiple vars missing (batch listing), and empty required list (edge case). This covers the core behavioral surface of the function.

suggestions.assertion: Looks for relevant patterns in the codebase or skill instructions instead of inventing the design in isolation.
suggestions.reason: When no codebase is provided, this assertion becomes untestable for the 'codebase' half. A run that fabricates an architecture with no grounding could still nominally pass. Consider splitting into two assertions: one for codebase-present runs (checks referenced paths against actual files) and one for codebase-absent runs (checks that the plan explicitly documents its assumptions and instructs the implementing agent to verify them before writing code).

suggestions.assertion: Artifact metadata fields are concretely filled (no placeholder bracket values) for status, track, and next step.
suggestions.reason: The artifact contains '<package>' placeholders throughout the file paths in the Implementation Map and other sections. The assertion as written only checks status/track/next-step metadata fields — so it passes — but a reader scanning the artifact sees angle-bracket placeholders everywhere. Consider adding a separate assertion: 'Any placeholders in the artifact are explicitly documented as requiring resolution by the implementing agent, not silently left for the reader to infer.' This run handles it correctly (Next-Agent Kickoff step 2 calls this out), but the eval doesn't check for it.

No assertion verifies the code sketch in the Recommended Design section is syntactically valid and semantically correct Python. In this run the sketch is correct, but a future run could produce broken code that passes all current assertions.

eval_feedback.overall: Strong eval set that catches the meaningful outcomes. Two structural gaps worth addressing: the codebase-absent case for expectation 1, and the tolerance for undocumented placeholders in expectation 4.
